intelligence.raw_jd:
Software Engineer – Backend (Java Spring Boot) at Engineering

We are looking for a Backend Software Engineer with strong fundamentals in Java and Spring Boot to design, develop, and maintain scalable backend systems. The role involves building RESTful APIs, integrating third-party services such as payment gateways and messaging APIs, and collaborating with cross-functional teams to deliver reliable and efficient applications. The ideal candidate is hands-on, detail-oriented, and capable of working in a fast-paced development environment.

Desired Experience:
0–2 years of experience in backend development or internships Experience building RESTful APIs using Java and Spring Boot Exposure to real-world projects involving API integrations Familiarity with debugging, testing, and improving application performance Experience working in collaborative team environments

Responsibilities:
Design and develop scalable backend services using Java and Spring Boot Build and maintain RESTful APIs for seamless frontend-backend communication Integrate third-party APIs such as payment gateways (e.g., Easebuzz) and messaging platforms (e.g., WhatsApp Business API) Implement CRUD operations, filtering, and data validation mechanisms Debug and resolve production issues to improve system reliability Collaborate with frontend developers and cross-functional teams Write clean, maintainable, and efficient code Participate in code reviews and follow best engineering practices

Skills:
Strong proficiency in Java Hands-on experience with Spring Boot and Hibernate Experience building RESTful APIs Knowledge of relational databases (MySQL / PostgreSQL) Familiarity with Git and version control workflows Understanding of backend architecture and API design
Basic knowledge of frontend technologies (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React) Experience with API testing tools like Postman Exposure to payment gateway integrations Familiarity with messaging APIs (e.g., WhatsApp Business API) Understanding of software development lifecycle and agile methodologies

interview_trace.question: Can you describe a time when you had to troubleshoot and resolve a complex deployment issue in a production environment?
interview_trace.answer: Yeah, so there was this day when I deployed a Kubernetes server in the AWS architecture. And then the thing was, it was not going to that site, right? So the thing was, in the SD part, that is the security group, I hadn't mentioned the port which the application should go in. So that's why it was not, so I was not able to enter that site.

interview_trace.question: Can you walk me through how you identified the root cause of the issue and what steps you took to resolve it?
interview_trace.answer: So So I use the data dog mentioned mentioning how To find the specific failure point I used dog watch as well to shape the metrics like the laws were not filling right in and then I Used the I asked my friend. Yeah, I used up here in two reviews and all and along with that I used the unit testing as well. So why the application was not going in and that's how I

interview_trace.question: Can you walk me through how you configured the DogWatch alerting rules to notify you of the specific failure point, and what specific metrics did you use to trigger those alerts?
interview_trace.answer: To ensure that the dog watch alerts performed with the exact failure point, I relieved heavily on the robust tagging and grouping strategy when confirming the monitors and the alert message payload. And then, for the triggers myself, I focused on leading indicators, error rates, latency, and so on. And this is how I started to use those triggers.

interview_trace.question: Can you describe a situation where you had to optimize a Python application for performance?
interview_trace.answer: In the previous project, I was working on a data pipeline where a Python-based processing was taking too long to transform and load large datasets, causing a bottleneck in our daily reporting. So the actions which I took were the profiling. First, I didn't guess the problem. I used cprofile module to identify exactly what's the problem. And vectorization, instead of filtering through rows, I preferred the code to use the vectorized operations. And for multiprocessing, for the last, it was CPU-born, but independently, I used a multiprocessing library. And then for data annealing, I switched from reading entire service point to memory to using things from more efficient formats like Paracate, which reduced our memory, footprint, and I.O. wait times.

interview_trace.question: Can you walk me through a specific example of how you applied vectorization to improve performance in the data pipeline, and what kind of operations were you able to vectorize?
interview_trace.answer: Yeah, so in the recent data transformation task, I encountered a bottleneck where a script was calculating derived metrics across millions of rows using row by row for you, which was incredibly slow, right? So to optimize this, I refactored the logic to use NumPy and Pandas vectorized operations, which allowed the CPU to perform the same operation on an entire arrays at once using the highly optimized C and Fortran code. Specifically, I was able to replace conditional logic and arithmetic operations, such as calculating tax-adjusted tools based on multiple column conditions, which are vectorized, NP.WAP statements, and direct column-wise mathematical operations. So this is how the shift eliminated those things.

interview_trace.question: Can you walk me through how you handled the case where some of the columns used in the vectorized operations were missing values, and how you ensured the derived metrics were correctly calculated in those scenarios?
interview_trace.answer: Yeah, to handle missing values, I used Pandas methods like .filana to create nulls with sensible defaults, like zero for numeric calculations or mean median imputation before performing the vectorized operations. And alternatively, I used .pana to execute incomplete reports if they were statistically insignificant, or I utilized conditional logic with np.wire to handle missing cases, specifically ensuring they didn't propagate non-results into my final matrix. And this approach maintained data integrity while keeping the performance benefits of the vectorization method.

interview_trace.question: Can you describe a time when you had to troubleshoot and resolve a deployment issue in a production environment?
interview_trace.answer: Yeah, so in a recent production deployment, we encountered a service failure caused by a configuration mismatch between our containerized application and a secret management system. So I immediately identified the issue by analyzing the pod logs and monitoring metrics, which showed a crash loop backup due to an authentication error. I quickly implemented the rollback to the previous table, image to restore service availability, and then verified the environment variables in our CDI CD pipelines. I corrected the C grid mapping and successfully redeployed in our control staging test before pushing the fix to the production.

interview_trace.question: Can you walk me through the specific steps you took to verify the environment variables in your CD pipelines, and what tools or scripts you used to automate that process?
interview_trace.answer: Yeah. So to verify the environment variables, I integrated a validation stage directly into our CICID pipeline using a combination of ENV substantial for template testing and custom pipeline scripts. During the build phase, these scripts would face our configuration file like .env or Kubernetes config maps and correspondence them against the source of code, schema, missing or mal-informed keys before deployment. To automate this, I utilized Genkin pipelines to plug into inject and scan these values and use kubectl dryer and commands to edit the final manifest structure and ensuring that any discrepancies we've lagged immediately in the pipeline blocks to prevent quality configurations from ever reaching the production.

interview_trace.question: Can you walk me through how you handled edge cases, such as environment variables with special characters or non-string values, in your validation scripts?
interview_trace.answer: To handle special characters and non-string values, I ensure my validation script strictly enforced proper coding and typecasting. For special characters, dollar or exclamation, I used Base64 encoding during the storage and injection process to prevent shell injection or premature expansion. Recording them only at runtime. For non-string values, I implemented schema validation using JSON schema or pydent, which explicitly requires variables to be cast to the expected type, such as integers or booleans, before the requirement process. Ensuring that any type mismatch triggers an immediate pipeline failure rather than a runtime error.
